1 EDMS 20-500-1 Issue: FEB-2021 / Rev- 1 TECHNICAL SPECIFICATION FOR HEAD END SYSTEM

2 CONTENTS NO Description Page 1 Head End System - General Requirements 3 2 Head End System - Meter Data Readings 5 3 Head End System - Metering Field Operations and Maintenance 6 4 Head End System – Reports 6 5 Head End System – Security 7 6 Head End System - Utility Operator User Interface 8 7 Head End System - Integrated GIS Tool 8 8 Head End System - MDMS Interface 9

3 Head End System 1. Head End System - General Requirements: The Head End System (HES) shall process 350 Million Messages in 2 hours for demand response (connect and disconnect relay operations) and Interval Data. 1.1. The Head End System shall process 140 Million Messages between itself and the Meter Data Management System (MDMS) in 6-8 hours 1.2. The Head End System shall act as the central collection point for the data within the AMI system. The HES collects data from all of the Data Concentrators and stores the gathered data in a secure database. Once data is stored and analyzed on the server, the data shall be available for display via an easy-to-use, web-based graphical interface. 1.3. The Supplier shall provide the hardware and software required to run the HES

including any elements to monitor, backup and restore the system. 1.4. The Head End System shall manage and archive data for five years such that it can be accessed by any Utility computer or handheld devices, both locally and remotely via the web. 1.5. Mandatory - All data used within the solution shall be the sole property of the Distribution Company (DISCO), as cannot be used by, shared with or demonstrated to any third party. Any misuse of this data will be subject to the Egyptian Law. 1.6. The Head End System software must be web browser-based and shall have defined applications with standard interfaces to allow for existing and planned software applications. 1.7. The Head End System software must have flexible meter reading data output

formats that are compatible with the DISCOs current billing application 1.8. The HES shall have the ability to pass along alerts and status codes to upstream systems for publication to the consumers as configured by the HES user interface. 1.9. Each meter-generated alert shall be accompanied by a duration the alert has been active that shall be stored and optionally sent out by the server. 1.10. For Managed Hosting Solutions, the Host Server shall be on an open system platform and support multiple operating systems, and shall work with any database system server

4 software such as that provided by Microsoft, Oracle, etc., and managed by the DISCO Personnel in cooperation with Vender Support. 1.11. For Perpetual License Solutions, the Suppler shall provide to the DISCO a database software server license. 1.12. The Host Software shall communicate with the Data Concentrator / WAN Operated meters via the open, international communication protocol, Device Language Message Specification (DLMS) / Companion Specification for Energy Metering (COSEM). Alternative solutions for the communication protocol shall be considered on merit. 1.13. The DLMS/COSEM profile(s) (data structure) will be defined, programmed, and tested during the proof-of-concept and pilot deployments. If alternative solutions have been

provided in accordance with 3.1.13, the Supplier shall define, program, and test the profiles for those solutions. These definitions, programming, and testing shall be at no additional cost to the Utility. 1.14. Mandatory - The Head End System software shall have a commercially and practically proven track record, with a reference list of multiple deployed AMI projects of significantly size (at least 1 Million meters/endpoints) working for at least 2 years, accompanied by satisfaction letters from the project owners. At least 3 letters from different utilities are required. 1.15. The Head End System shall have a Certificate of Operation with different electricity meter/ Data Concentrators Suppliers (at least 3 meter Suppliers); this certificate shall

be addressed from the successful project owners. 1.16. The Head End System should have a Certificate of successful integration with at least two reputable MDMS software solutions; this certificate shall be addressed from the successful project owners. 1.17. Mandatory - The bidder shall provide standard Multi Speak interfaces for integration to a future Meter Data Management System (MDMS), CRM, Outage Management System (OMS), and a Prepayment System (PPS) from the Head End System The successful bidder will also be able to support additional Multi Speak interfaces to additional systems in the future. 1.18. The successful bidder shall be able to submit their head end system (HES) to the Multi Speak testing / Bodies for certification during the evaluation period and pass the

certification tests for the identified interfaces. 1.19. The HES software shall be in English and Arabic Language interface, with associated applications elements, menus, online help file, printed documentation, and training materials.

5 2. Head End System - Meter Data Readings: 2.1. Head End System shall provide on-demand and scheduled reading of Smart Meters 2.1.1. Date and time 2.1.2. Energy reading profile data 2.1.3. Event log 2.1.4. Firmware version 2.1.5. Serial Number 2.1.6. Type 2.1.7. Other data points as configured by the DISCO (e.g., voltage, demand, status flags, etc.) 2.2. Head End System shall support two reading mechanisms, either pull or push operation, or a combination between them. 2.2.1. Pull operation means that the reading process will be initiated and controlled by the HES. 2.2.2. Push Operation means that a Data Concentrator / WAN Operated Smart Meter sends its data proactively to the Head End System, based on the following conditions:

2.2.2.1. Predefined Schedule, which previously configured in the Data Concentrator / WAN Operated Smart Meter. 2.2.2.2. Vital information (Data Concentrator / WAN Operated Smart Meter Alarms, Smart Meters newly registered). 2.2.3. The Reading Process shall be done on a batch of meters, or for a larger collection of Meters according to predefine Reading Process and Procedures 2.2.3.1. In case of non-complete reading data, the Head End System shall perform the reading process on the failed devices several times with different predefined interval time. 2.2.4. Head End System shall provide a flexible reading schedule that enables repetitions a nd start delays 2.2.5. Head End System shall provide a statistical analysis of the quality of communication

performance. 2.2.6. Head End System shall provide a minimum set of System performance counters 2.2.6.1. Number of executed tasks 2.2.6.2. Number of failed tasks 2.2.6.3. Number of successful tasks 2.2.6.4. Maximum tasks performed per hour 2.2.6.5. Average execution time for scheduled reading tasks 2.2.7. Head End System shall provide an estimated reading performance before performing the Reading process, in order to facilitate decision making by the Utility operator.

6 3. Head End System - Metering Field Operations and Maintenance: 3.1. Head End System shall support the processes and procedures of installation, replacement, and removal 3.1.1. For electricity meters 3.1.2. For Data Concentrators 3.2. Head End System shall provide static and dynamic grouping of devices: 3.2.1. Placing devices in static groups according to geographic regions, transformers, substations, etc. 3.2.2. Dynamic groups based on defined selection criteria. 3.2.3. These groups shall be automatically updated after installation, replacement, and removal devices. 3.3. Head End System shall provide a mechanism batch import of devices using device templates 3.3.1. These templates shall be verified and approved by the Agency.

3.4. The Head End System shall detect the newly installed meter / Data Concentrator, and label its Status with “Installed“ 3.5. The Head End System shall configure the newly installed meter / Data Concentrators with the appropriate parameters 3.5.1. Tariff Scheme 3.5.2. Date and Time 3.5.3. Load Limit 3.5.4. Others as defined by the DISCO (e.g., encryption keys, demand limits, etc.) 4. Head End System – Reports: 4.1. The Host Software shall be used to generate reports; view demand graphs, determine usage patterns and enforce energy restrictions. 4.2. The reports shall be available in graphical and tabular views for reading and consumption for various intervals. 4.3. The graphs shall be available to show high/low temperatures for each day and rainfall

7 4.4. The reporting feature shall support multiple report generation/export formats, minimally 4.4.1. Extensible Markup Language (XML) 4.4.2. Comma-separated Values (CSV) 4.4.3. Microsoft Excel 4.4.4. Microsoft Word 4.4.5. Adobe PDF 5. Head End System – Security: 5.1. Head E nd Sy stem s hall provide secured communication internally (among its components) and externally (to metering devices and upper-level systems). 5.2. Head End System shall support user and group security rights and polices for authentication, which based on the rights it grants access to its database and features: 5.2.1. Right to access Specific Data 5.2.2. Right to execute operations {reading, disconnection, reconnection, etc.}

5.2.3. Manage Devices. 5.2.4. Add/Remove/Edit User Accounts 5.3. The Head End System shall provide a secure remote access mechanism, log in and password. 5.4. The Head End System Software shall use a method for encryption/decryption of data exchanged between the Data Concentrator / WAN Operated Smart Meters and the Head End System. 5.4.1. At least ECC192 method shall be used in all information exchanges in public network as well as between concentrator and Head End System directly. 5.4.2. The security method and hardware should be described in details by the Bidder. 5.4.3. The HES shall be capable of supporting additional encryption methods over the life cycle of the system – it is expected that by the time this system is retired 256 bit keys will be required.

5.4.4. The HES shall be capable of managing encryption key rotation for the meters it communicates with. 5.4.5. The HES shall provide role-based access control. 5.5. The Bidder shall prove following items: 5.5.1. Security of method 5.5.2. Utility’s operator ability to change security key remotely 5.5.3. Confidentiality of remote key changing 5.5.4. All input and output information decrypted and encrypted

8 6. Head End System - Utility Operator User Interface: 6.1. The Head End System solution shall provide secure web-based user interface to the Utility personnel in order to facilitate their working procedures. 6.2. The User Interface shall permit the sending of alert outages, tampering, out -of- bounds system operating parameters to appropriate utility personnel via cell phone, pager, or e-mail. 6.3. The User Interface shall allow the utility to correlate consumption with meteorological data available on the user interface. 6.4. The Consumer data shall be available via a user -friendly interface that will allow for analysis, as well as bill generation. 6.5. The HES will support role based security for users

6.6. The HES will support a minimum of Arabic and English languages in the interface. Switching between languages shall be performed by setting parameters in the user login script. 6.7. Setting up user accounts will be done via a user -friendly web -linked interface by the properly authorized administrator-role user. 7. Head End System - Integrated GIS Tool: 7.1. The Host Software shall include a graphical information system (GIS) tool for network routing from Data Concentrators to AMI meters. 7.2. The GIS tool shall have icons that permit viewing system health of the star/mesh system components. 7.3. Routing distances from Data Concentrators to meters shall be part of the GIS tool. 7.4. The GIS tool shall allow monitoring of performance and loading on the utility’s
Infrastructure with the ability to display this information on GIS maps.

9 7.5. The GIS tool shall allow monitoring of electric meter outage/restoration detection for electric meters. 8. Head End System - MDMS Interface 8.1. Head End System should provide multiple web service methods for being integrated with any MDMS and other Systems. The HES shall comply the Multi Speak protocol /Standard interface for messages and methods. 8.2. The awar ded bidder sh all have the respo nsibility to i ntegrate with the Syst em Integrator ( MDM, Bi lling, Prep aid, Ven ding, etc.), t hat will be awarded in the subsequent RFP. 8.3. All interfaces from the HES to the MDMS systems shall conform to Multi Speak interface standards. 8.4. The Interface shall minimally provide a functionality level of:

8.4.1. Metering Devices Management (e.g. Connection/disconnections, Load Control of AMI meters) 8.4.2. Triggering scheduled readings of metering data. 8.4.3. Triggering on-demand readings of metering data 8.4.4. Deploying Configuration files to meters and Data Concentrators (Tariff Schemes, Load Limits, etc.) 8.4.5. Provide operational status notifications to the MDMS (e .g., meter reading status working / finished) 8.4.6. Provide Push Notification alarms or events to MDMS. 8.4.7. Vending (Postpaid / Prepaid) Related operation data.

Page 3 of 4 1 Meter Enclosures - General Requirements This Enclosures Specification covers manufacturing, testing, and supply of Meter Enclosures for three-phase direct connected meters. a- The Meter Enclosure shall be made of insulating material intended to contain meters to be installed on walls or similar flat surface structures and include a Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB). b- The Meter Enclosure shall be weather proof and ultraviolet resistant (to within type test limits, at minimum). c- The Meter Enclosure will be supplied with the MCB and the MCB will be mounted inside the Meter Enclosure in such a way as to permit access to the MCB lever without opening the Meter Enclosure cover. d- The Meter Enclosures shall be compatible with the supplied Smart Meter case dimensions for the

required installation configurations. 1.1. Service Condition 1.1.1 The M eter Enclosures to be supplied according to this specification shall be suitable for satisfactory continuous operation under the following environmental conditions. Condition Specification 1 Maximum ambient temperature (°C) 70 2 Minimum ambient temperature (°C) -5 3 Maximum daily average temperature (°C) 35 4 Maximum annual average temperature (°C) 30 9 Average relative humidity (%) 80 10 Maximum wind velocity (km/h) 180 1.2. Plastic Material and Construction Requirement a) The base and cover of the Meter Enclosure shall be made of injection molded Polycarbonate with the cover fully transparent. The base of the Meter Enclosure shall be grey in color. The thickness shall be minimum 3 mm for

three phase. b) The Meter Enclosure for Pole mounted transformer shall have IP 54, and inclined roof not less than 5˚. c) The Meter Enclosure shall have a tapered roof for easy flow of rainwater. d) The Meter Enclosure shall have suitable holes of diameter 25mm2 at the bottom / side for entry of serv ice connection wires fixed with two rubber grommets. e) The Meter Enclosure must be unbreakable, and shall withstand temperatures up to 125°C, certified and warranted by the manufacturer. f) Clearance of minimum 10 mm shall be maintained from top and both sides of the meter. However, the clearance at bottom will be minimum 60 mm for the phase including the MCB.

Page 4 of 4 g) The overall dimensions (in mm) of the Meter Enclosure will be as per the dimensions mentioned above considering bidders own meter. h) For holding and locking the cover, U-shaped latches with one hole for reverting on the base and other for sealing the Meter Enclosure shall be provided. This clamp will also clamp the cover with base. The total number of clamps for each Meter Enclosure will be two. In addition, sealing holes of 3 to 3.5 mm diameter will be provided in U clamps. i) The Meter Enclosure shall have two hinges. j) The Meter Enclosure shall be provided with four installation/attachment holes of 8 mm dia. k) The meter base support shall be raised by about 10 mm in the Enclosure for easy wiring.

Soft rubberized gasket for protection on all around base shall be provided. l) The Meter Enclosure shall meet IP 54 for dust and water protection as per IEC 60529:1989. The following information shall be clearly embossed on the Meter Enclosures m) Distribution System Company (DISCO) Name n) The Meter Enclosure shall have an enough space for placing the consumer identification card, which contains meter identification number (e.g., serial number), customer name, and connection registration number on front of the Meter Enclosure cover. 1.3. Tests and Compliance standards 1.3.1 General – The tests shall be made at the prevailing ambient temperature unless specified otherwise in the relevant clause.

1.3.2 Categories of Test- The test shall constitute type and acceptance tests as enumerated below. No. Standard Description 1 IEC 60670-1(1989) The Meter Enclosure shall be designed per IEC, i.e. the engineering plastic used shall not support combustion (Fire Retardant). Type Tests (a) Test for dimensions (b) Test for Mechanical strength. (c) Test for water absorption. (d) Test for stability at high temperature. 2 IEC 60898-1 (2002) Test for resistance to abnormal Heat and fire. Glow wire test at 650 deg. as per Publication No. IEC 60695-2-11 3 IEC 60323 Fire retardant test and test for self-extinguishing Property. 4 IEC 60707-1981 Test for determination of flammability of solid electrical insulating

materials when exposed to igniting sources. Material shall confirm to category FVI. 5 IEC 61338 Verification of di-electric properties. 6 Insulation Test With 500V Megger. Insulation test with 500 VDC Megger for one -minute insulation should be 100 M ohms.

4 Smart Meters- single phase Direct Meter 1. General Requirements 1.1. Scope Many important business processes conducted by the DISCOs require re-engineering to increase effectiveness and efficiency. An important enabler for this re-engineering is an Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI). The functionality of the AMI can contribute to a large extent of the business needs identified by EEHC, Smart meters is the key role in AMI. Smart meters shall communicate using FAN telecommunications technologies such as PLC, PLC/RF, mobile wireless service 2G/3G/4G technology and RS 485. The choice of FAN telecommunication technologies shall optimize system design and architecture, interoperability, total cost of ownership while meeting

Specifications. 1.2. Service Conditions The meters to be supplied according to this specification shall be suitable for satisfactory continuous operation under the following environmental conditions: Table (1) 1.3. Environmental Requirements The meters shall conform to the environmental requirements as defined in IEC 62052-11, 62053-21 and 62053-23. In addition to those requirements, the following minimum requirements shall be met: 1.3.1. The meter shall be protected against malfunction due to the ingress of vermin, dust, or excess humidity by conformal coating of the printed circuit boards in the meter. 1.3.2. Any openings shall be as small as practically possible to prevent the ingress of dirt and vermin

and to limit the potential for vandalism or tamper following Meter case. 2. Standards and codes of practice: 2.1. All material and equipment supplied and all works carried out shall comply in every respect with the technical codes of the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and with the recommendations of the International Electro technical Commissio n (IEC) and the European Standards (EN), which apply to the electrical equipment. 8 5. General Functional Requirements 5.1. Meter Sealing 5.1.1 Provision shall be for sealing the meter with at least two Ferrule stainless steel seals 5.1.2 The stainless steel seals shall be applied in such a way that it will not be possible to undo/loosen the mounting screws used to secure the meter without breaking these seals. 5.1.3 The stainless steel seals shall be applied in such a way that they will be easily visible when viewing an installed meter from the front. 5.1.4 The terminals shall also be having Ferrule/TT sealing arrangement. 5.2. Meter Local Communication Interface 5.2.1 The meter shall have an IEC 62056-21 compliant optical communication port. This shall be place to allow the Utility to access via the front of the meter for a variety of interactions via a

hand held unit (HHU). 5.2.2. The meter shall have an additional 2-wire serial communication port, RS485 compatible with IEC 62056 communication protocol for: a. Local connected network of maximum 30 meters with one wide area network (WAN) Gateway. b. Local connection with nearest data concentrator in the field of installation. 5.3. Meter Remote Communication Interface 5.3.1 The meter shall have remote communication PLC, PLC/RF, mobile wireless service 2G/3G/4G technology and communication option which will be able to interface with RS-485, RJ-45 port for data communication with the central server from meters via one of the different communication media 5.3.2 In case of cellular Communication modem shall use at least 4G mobile network

communications. 5.3.3 The communication modules works as a plug-and-play model (modular), without the need to modify/change the meter firmware. 5.3.4 The communication protocol shall be compatible with the IEC 62056 Dynamic DLMS/COSEM Application Layer Protocol. 5.3.5 The bidder shall submit the range(s) of frequencies that he can support with his RF ba sed metering system. The bidder shall also submit his RF related specifications, including Access Points and Bridges, Antennas, Maximum Output Power, Transmit and Receive Intervals, Mean Root-Square Emission Levels, etc. 5.3.6 The DLMS/COSEM profile(s) will be defined, programmed, and tested during pilot deployments. These definitions, programming, and testing shall be at no additional cost to the

9 6 Firmware upgrade 6.1 Mandatory - The Utility shall be able to update / change the meter firmware locally via the optical port or other ports installed on the meter. 6.2 The firmware upgrade mechanism shall be designed so as not to alter in any way the metering characteristics (metrology) or the meter data stored in the meter: a) Meter data b) Meter status c) Configuration parameters or operational parameters All of these data must remain unchanged even after firmware upgrades. 6.3 New firmware will be submitted to the meter with date and time parameter of executing new firmware operation (the meter will upload the new updated firmware but will only execute it when the defined date and time

parameter achieved). 6.4 The meter will record the date and time of receipt of new firmware in the event log, as well as the date and time of the firmware upgrade process execution. 6.5 The meter will perform self-check process after the execution of the new firmware update, and the result of self - check process will be stored on the meter event log, and shall be retrievable locally or remotely. 6.6 The meter shall have non-volatile memory equal to or greater than ten times the size of the operational/running firmware image. 6.7 The meter shall support two concurrent firmware images: current operational/running and pending upgrade. 7 Security 7.1 The meters shall use a method for encryption/decryption of data exchanged in all meter interfaces.

According to cyber security department penetration test. 7.2 Mandatory - A method employing minimally AES128-CBC/GCM shall be used in all i nformation exchanges in private network, between meter and data concentrator (Field Area Network, or FAN interface), and between the meter and hand held unit (HHU) via the optical interface. Enough reserve capacity in the components of the system (e.g., main processor, memory) shall exist to support AES256-CBC/GCM during the service life of the equipment. 7.3 The meter shall support encryption and decryption of two meter firmware images: current operational/running, pending upgrade> 7.4 The meter should be capable of supporting AES256-CBC/GCM during its operational lifetime without a

hardware change. 7.5 A method employing at least ECC 256 shall be used in all information exchanges on public network, as well as between meter and central system directly. 7.6 The Bidder shall describe the security mecha nism, including encryption/decryption methods, for the exchange of secure tokens or credits while the meter is in prepayment mode. 7.7 The security methods and hardware shall be described in details by bidder. 7.8 The bidder shall prove following items: a) Security of method b) Client ability to change security key remotely

10 c) Confidentiality of remote key changing d) If client changes the security key there is no way for meter manufacturers to retrieve new key (New key is not accessible for meter hardware, firmware, and external software) e) All input and output information decrypted and encrypted 8 Meter Operational Modes The meter shall be required to support at least two modes of operation: Normal Mode and Test Mode. 8.1 Meter Test Mode 8.1.1 The meter shall support a Test Mode that activated by using the optical port. 8.1.2 In Test Mode, there shall be an automated test sequence available that includes: a) Full diagnostic test, that testing of the all the active and inactive functionality b) Metering accuracy test (enabled to allow for accuracy testing to be performed without affecting the

recorded customer registration for energy) 8.1.3 The Test Mode shall also have the following displayed information (scrolled sequence): a) Meter firmware version b) Meter firmware update date and time stamp c) Meter tampering events d) Meter status e) Current limit f) Connect/Disconnect switch/Latching Relay open and close count times 9 Load Profile 9.1 At least four programmable channels for: a. Active interval energy (import and export) b. Active cumulative energy (import and export) Adjustable time interval from 1 to 60 minutes. Meter shall store these parameters for at least 30 days. 9.2 At least four programmable channels shall be for voltage and current as well as active power. Measurement type for each channel can be chosen as follows:

a. average b. minimum c. maximum Adjustable time interval from 1 to 60 minutes. Meter shall store these parameters for at least 30 days. 9.3 Time & date shall be included in load profile. 9.4 Load profile shall be readable in complete form and in defined blocks (based on start and end time and channels) locally and remotely.

11 9.5 Historic Consumption For this option, there are twelve energy registers store the total energy consumed for the previous twelve months. These registers are cycled at during the month-end process, with the oldest data being deleted. 9.6 Date and time management 9.6.1 The meter shall be equipped with a real time clock supporting the date and time. Pseudo-clock using mains crossing detection is not acceptable. The maximum drift of the clock shall be less than 30 seconds per month. The battery shall operate maintenance-free for a period of at least 10 years. 9.6.2 The meter shall be equipped with a battery monitoring system that monitors the battery’s condition and sends a “Change Battery Alarm” message to the HES when the battery approaches the end of its

operating life 9.6.3 When first commissioned into the AMI System the meter shall synchronize its time & date with that of the HES. Meter time & date synchronization shall also be done on a periodic basis through the HES, with a programmable periodicity, and done automatically during meter installation stage. 9.7 Power quality 9.7.1 Meter shall record under voltage, overvoltage, and power cut. 9.7.2 Events related to both under voltage and overvoltage shall be recorded in the meter. Threshold of under voltage is from 210 to 132 by 5V decrements and for overvoltage is from 230 to 285 by 5V increments 9.7.3 The voltage events will not be recorded unless they continue for a time equal to or greater than the

programmable time set for under voltage and overvoltage threshold. This time shall be adjustable / programmable between 1 to 60 seconds, in 1-second increments. 9.7.4 For each under voltage, minimum voltage occurred during the adjustable / Programmable (1 to 60) Second period, the meter shall be detected and recorded as such; date and time of last occurrence and the cumulative number of the occurrences. 9.7.5 For each overvoltage, max. Voltage occurred and during the adjustable / Programmable (1 to 60) Second period the meter shall be detected and recorded as such; date and time of last occurrence and the cumulative number of the occurrences. 9.7.6 Parameters related to threshold and duration of under voltage and overvoltage shall be adjustable

locally and remotely. 9.7.7 Events related to power cut shall be recorded in the meter as follows: a) For long duration power cuts (more than 3 min.), duration time and date shall be recorded. b) For short duration power cuts, number of power cuts shall be recorded. 10 Meter Display 10.1 Meter display shall display all measured quantities or desired quantity automatically or scrolling via button on the meter as follows: a) Active energy in each tariff b) Current tariff

12 c) Max. Demand in each tariff, and the highest Max. Demand with exact time & date d) Time and date e) Serial number f) Battery failure g) The number of voltage cut offs and number of reverse energy events h) Contactor’s status i) All measured parameters shall be displayed (including instantaneous quantities, cumulative energy values, and maximum demand in all tariffs with time & date indication). 10.2 Meter shall have capability of display and record of current & pervious Max. Demand with ability of configuration of Max. Demand time interval calculation. Max. Demand shall be measured at least in three integer a nd two decimal digits. 10.3 LCD shall display measured items in OBIS codes. 10.4 Meter Display shall support Arabic language Interface).

11 Fault & Status Display The meter shall provide a visible indication of the status of the incoming supply. 11.1 Status/Alarm/Event Displays In addition to the displays required above, the meter must display the following minimum information in an intuitive way such that an inexperienced user can understand and interpret the information intelligibly (laminated instruction cards are to be provided for each unit): 1. Meter ID 2. Out of Credit message (for prepayment meters only) 3. Meter tamper state 4. Meter failure and/or fault code The bidder must submit a detailed specification of the way in which the displays are structured and laid out.

15 15 Tamper detection 15.1 The meter shall classify and record the following commonly-occurring tamper events with a date and time stamp. The tampering event log shall record any combination of the following possible tamper conditions: Table (8) 15.2 Tamper shall be detected with and without mains power connections. When the meter detects a tamper condition, it shall be capable of configuring the latching relay/contactor to perform an action or not on the connected loads with an appropriate message. 15.3 There shall be a mechanism by which the consumer can be disconnected immediately when a verifiable tamper event occurs. 15.4 The tamper status shall be reset by using the meter optical port.

15.5 The meter shall send a push message to the Data Concentrator or even to the central system (HES) with every Tamper Event, stamped with date and time. 15.6 The meter shall provide tamper log data with the last occurred tamper date and time stamp and it can be easily readable remotely or locally. 16 Meter Metrology 16.1 The meter shall have the ability of measuring and recording import/export active energy (The values to be recorded for import and export are actual values). 16.2 The measuring quantity for energy shall have following resolutions: a. for Active Energy (Min 0.01 kWh resolution). b. for Reactive Energy (Min 0.01 kVARh resolution) 16.3 The meter shall measure import/export energy as well as Absolute value of active energy and it shall be possible

to activate this capability. 16.4 The meter shall measure phase current (True RMS) 16.5 The meter shall measure phase voltage (True RMS) 16.6 The meter shall measure Power Factor. 16.7 The measuring and recording maximum demand of active power shall be based on configurable time intervals and subintervals (sliding mode). a. It shall be possible to reset maximum demand remotely Tamper Case Single phase, direct- connected Current Reverse Phase and or current reverse Current imbalance Ip ≠ In (fully earth or partial earth connection, bypass connection) Voltage Missing √ External magnetic field influence √ Opening meter cover √ Opening terminal cover √

16 b. It shall be possible to reset maximum demand locally via sealed push button on front of the meter. c. It shall be possible automatically Maximum demand to be reset at specific billing Period. Above capability shall be activated (or not) and all parameter shall be adjustable remotely and locally. 16.8 The meter shall measure the energy from both phase and neutral (which one is larger) by same accuracy. 16.9 Voltage sampling shall be done before input of the contactor/ latching relay. 17 Payment Mode 17.1 Most benefits of prepayment mode is that credit is transferred to the meter from online vending method, and the amount credit in local currency provided/ stored inside the meter, the meter will provide

supply energy to the consumer when he have a enough credit. The credit reg ister is decremented against energy consumption with the countdown total being displayed on the meter display. 17.2 When the consumer has consumed their credit amount, the meter shall shut off the supplied energy to the consumer until the meter has been recharged with more credit. This shutting off process will not be activated during holidays, weekends, or defined “Friendly Hours” (utility non-working hours). 17.3 There shall be a mechanism to trigger Friendly Hours to block customer disconnection when AMI communication is lost for a definable period (in hours) but electric service is maintained so that the customer will not lose service due to lack of credit.

17.4 For post-payment mode, the meter will calculate the consumed energy according to the tariff management system, and transfer into money payable value, which shall then be displayed on the meter display in a local currency value. 17.5 Under normal circumstances, the consumer will pay their bill within the defined grace period as indicated on the bill (or by some other mechanism). Notifications about payment due shall be via SMS message, paper bills, or Utility personnel, among others. In the case of non -payment over the period as determined by the Utility for their collections process and programmed into the meter (number of day of grace period), the meter will shut off the supplied energy to the consumer. Optionally, there shall be a two -

stage demand limiting function whereby the Utility can configure two lower demand limits to be managed by the meter and switch to encourage the customer to manage their consumption and ultimately pay for it. 17.6 Mandatory - The meter shall be capable to be switched from pre payment to post -payment mode remotely from the Master Information Center / Central System and locally via the optical port 18 Credit register (prepayment mode only) The credit register is the “operating account” of the meter being decremented by the metering process. The specific requirements of this register are: 18.1 This register must be decremented for the equivalent of every credit unit consumed. 18.2 When the credit reaches a pre -determined point, the customer shall be informed about the remaining balance

and advised to purchase more credit. Optionally, a soft demand limit is enacted, permitting them to maintain service at a lower level to stretch the remaining credit until more is purchased. There shall be two programmable points to enable this process at two different load levels.

17 18.3 When the available credit has been consumed, the meter must open the contactor and isolate supply from the customer (unless the Friendly hours are invoked). 18.4 The balance of this register shall be collected by the HES. 19 Friendly Hours, Weekend & Holiday Features 19.1 Meter shall accommodate the “Friendly hours”, “Weekend”, and “Holidays” features. These are periods during which the meter shall not cut-off power to consumer if the credit becomes negative. The Friendly Hour mechanism shall also be configurable to be triggered due to loss of AMI communications for a defined length of time. 19.2 Details of the features are described in the following table: Table (9) No. Requirement Description

1 Friendly Hours There shall be provision to define ‘Friendly hours’ in the meter. The utility shall be able to define the ‘Friendly hours’ and shall be communicated to the meter through token. 2 Weekend There shall be provision to define ‘Weekend’ in the meter. The utility shall be able to define the weekend and shall be communicated to the meter through token. 3 Holidays There shall be provision to define ‘Holidays’ in the meter. At the end of a year, the utility shall be able to define new holiday dates and these shall be communicated to meter through token 20 Tariff 20.1 All tariff calculation and credit calculation shall be processed inside the meter. 20.2 The meter shall support stepped tariffs structures as well as time -of-use and maximum

demand. 20.3 The tariff structure consists of the following records: a) Each tariff must be uniquely identified using the tariff code b) Each tariff shall have an activation date, being the date on which the tariff becomes effective. c) Each tariff has up to ten steps / time slot (in kWh) for different levels of energy pricing. d) The rate describes the cost per kWh for energy consumption in that step. 21 Tariff switchover When the meter detects that a new tariff is applicable (using the tariff activation date), the meter shall execute the following steps: . a) The meter shall begin the billing against the new tariff. b) The current tariff code shall be updated to reflect the new tariff code.
c) The old (expired) tariff should be deleted. d) New tariffs will only be activated at 00:00 on the first day of a month.

18 22 Billing Billing operates on a “monthly” basis (a period of days definable by the Utility). The energy consumption charge is deducted within the meter for at least every 1 kWh. All other charges including service charge, minimum charge, VAT, and other taxes are deducted at the POS. 23. Load Disconnection 23.1. The load shall be disconnected by a latching relay/contactor under the following conditions: a) The credit in the meter has expired and grace facilities have also expired b) The load power threshold has been exceeded c) The meter is in a tampered state 23.2. The meter shall indicate whether the load is connected or not and shall display the reason for disconnection. 23.3. Shall have the option to switch the contactor manually. Under over – current (reaching the load limit)

conditions by pushing a button in front of the meter, and also the mechanism shall permit automatic load reconnection and shall ensure that the number of switching cycles is limited. The meter shall attempt to reconnect the load u p to programmable trail times with programmable time period intervals. If the over - current condition still exits the meter shall wait a programmable period of time before attempting to reconnect the load , and after the limited times of switching cycles Expiration the meter will not restore back the energy unless it done remotely from the HES or even locally by the Optical port. 24. Load Control Meters shall have the options to control loads that shall be remotely activated or deactivated with the

following features: Table (10) 25. Smart Meters - Testing and Approval 25.1. 3 Smart Meters- Three Phase Direct Meter 1. General Requirements 1.1. Scope Many important business processes conducted by the DISCOs require re-engineering to increase effectiveness and efficiency. An important enabler for this re -engineering is an Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI). The functionality of the AMI can contribute to a large extent of the bu siness needs identified by EEHC, Smart meters is the key role in AMI. Smart meters shall communicate using FAN telecommunications technologies such as PLC, PLC/RF, mobile wireless service 2G/3G / 4G Technology , and RS 485. The choice of FAN telecommunication technologies shall optimize system design and architecture, interoperability, total cost of ownership while meeting

Specifications. 1.2. Service Conditions The meters to be supplied according to this specification shall be suitable for satisfactory continuous operation under the following environmental conditions: + Table (1) 1.3. Environmental Requirements The meters shall conform to the environmental requirements as defined in IEC 62052-11, 62053-21 and 62053-23. In addition to those requirements, the following minimum requirements shall be met: 1. The meter shall be protected against malfunction due to the ingress of vermin, dust, or excess humidity by conformal coating of the printed circuit boards in the meter. 2. Any openings shall be as small as practically possible to prevent the ingress of dirt and vermin

and to limit the potential for vandalism or tamper following Meter case. NO Condition Specification 1 Maximum ambient temperature ( ͦ C) 70 2 Minimum ambient temperature ( ͦ C) -5 3 Maximum daily average temperature ( ͦ C) 35 4 Maximum annual average temperature ( ͦ C) 30

7 5. General Functional Requirements 5.1. Meter Sealing 1. Provision shall be for sealing the meter with at least two Ferrule stainless steel seals 2. The stainless steel seals shall be applied in such a way that it will not be possible to undo/loosen the mounting screws used to secure the meter without breaking these seals. 3. The stainless steel seals shall be applied in such a way that they will be easily visible when viewing an installed meter from the front. 4. The terminals shall also be having Ferrule/TT sealing arrangement. 5.2. Meter Local Communication Interface 5.2.1. The meter shall have an IEC 62056 -21 compliant optical communication port. This shall be place to allow the Utility to access via the front of the meter for a variety of interactions via a hand held unit

(HHU). 5.2.2. The meter shall have an additional 2 -wire serial communication port, RS485 compatible with IEC 62056 communication protocol for: a. Local connected network of maximum 30 meters with one wide area network (WAN) Gateway. b. Local connection with nearest data concentrator in the field of installation. 5.3. Meter Remote Communication Interface 5.3.1. The meter shall have remote communication PLC, PLC/RF, mobile wireless service 2G/3G / 4G Technology and communication option which will be able to interface with RS -485, RJ -45 port for data communication with the central server from meters via one of the different communication media 5.3.2. In case of cellular Communication modem shall use at least 4G mobile network communications.

5.3.3. The communication modules works as a plug -and-play model (modular), without the need to modify/change the meter firmware. 5.3.4. The communication protocol shall be compatible with the IEC 62056 Dynamic DLMS/COSEM Application Layer Protocol. 5.3.5. The bidder shall submit the range(s) of frequencies that he can support with his RF based metering system. The bidder shall also submit his RF related specifications, including Access Points and Bridges, Antennas, Maximum Output Power, Transmit and Receive Intervals, Mean Root -Square Emission Levels, etc. 5.3.6. The DLMS/COSEM profile(s) wil l be defined, programmed, and tested during pilot deployments. These definitions, programming, and testing shall be at no additional cost to the Utility.
6. Firmware upgrade Mandatory - The Utility shall be able to update / change the meter firmware locally via the optical port or other ports installed on the meter.

8 The firmware upgrade mechanism shall be designed so as not to alter in any way the metering characteristics (metrology) or the meter data stored in the meter: 1. Meter data 2. Meter status 3. Configuration parameters or operational parameters All of these data must remain unchanged even after firmware upgrades. New firmware will be submitted to the meter with date and time parameter of e xecuting new firmware operation (the meter will upload the new updated firmware but will only execute it when the defined date and time parameter achieved). The meter will record the date and time of receipt of new firmware in the event log, as well as the date and time of the firmware upgrade process execution.

The meter will perform self-check process after the execution of the new firmware update, and the result of self-check process will be stored on the meter event log, and shall be retrievable locally or remotely. The meter shall have non-volatile memory equal to or greater than ten ti mes the size of the operational/running firmware image. The meter shall support two concurrent firmware images: current operational/running and pending upgrade. 7. Security 7.1 The meters shall use a method for encryption/decryption of data exchanged in all meter interfaces. According to cyber security department penetration test. 7.2 Mandatory - A method employing minimally AES128-CBC/GCM shall be used in all information exchanges in private

network, between meter and data concentrator (Field Area Network, or FAN interface), and between the meter and hand held unit (HHU) via the optical interface. Enough reserve capacity in the components of the system (e.g., main processor, memory) shall exist to support AES256-CBC/GCM during the service life of the equipment. 7.3 The meter shall support encryption and decryption of two meter firmware images: current operational/running, pending upgrade> 7.4 The meter should be capable of supporting AES256-CBC/GCM during its operational lifetime without a hardware change. 7.5 A method employing at least ECC 256 shall be used in all information exchanges on public network, as well as between meter and central system directly.

7.6 The Bidder shall describe the s ecurity mechanism, including encryption/decryption methods, for the exchange of secure tokens or credits while the meter is in prepayment mode. 7.7 The security methods and hardware shall be described in details by bidder. 7.8 The bidder shall prove following items: a) Security of method b) Client ability to change security key remotely

9 c) Confidentiality of remote key changing d) If client changes the security key there is no way for meter manufacturers to retrieve new key (New key is not accessible for meter hardware, firmware, and external software) e) All input and output information decrypted and encrypted 8. Meter Operational Modes The meter shall be required to support at least two modes of operation: Normal Mode and Test Mode. 8.1 Meter Test Mode The meter shall support a Test Mode that is activated by using the optical port. In Test Mode, there shall be an automated test sequence available that includes: 1. Full diagnostic test, that testing of the all the active and inactive functionality 2. Metering accuracy test (enabled to allow for accuracy testing to be performed without affecting the

recorded customer registration for energy) 3. Connection validation tests (three-phase sequence connection). The Test Mode shall also have the following displayed information (scrolled sequence): 1. Meter firmware version 2. Meter firmware update date and time stamp 3. Meter tampering events 4. Meter status 5. Current limit (this feature will not be implemented on transformer meters only) 6. Connect/Disconnect switch/Latching Relay open and close count times (this feature will not be implemented on transformer-operated meters) 9. Load Profile 9.1. At least four programmable channels for: a. Active interval energy (import and export) b. Reactive interval energy (import and export) * c. Active cumulative energy (import and export)
d. Reactive cumulative energy (import and export) * Adjustable time interval from 1 to 60 minutes.

10 Meter shall store these parameters for at least 30 days. 9.2. At least four programmable channels for voltage and current as well as active and reactive power. Measurement type for each channel can be chosen as follows: a. average b. minimum c. maximum Adjustable time interval from 1 to 60 minutes. Meter shall store these parameters for at least 30 days. 9.3. Time & date shall be included in load profile. 9.4. Load profile shall be readable in complete form and in defined blocks (based on start and end time and channels) locally and remotely. 9.5. Historic Consumption For this option, there are twelve energy registers that store the total energy consumed for the previous twelve months. These registers are cycled at during the month-end process, with the oldest data being deleted.

9.6. Date and time management 9.6.1 The meter shall be equipped with a real time clock supporting the date and time. Pseudo-clock using mains crossing detection is not acceptable. The maximum drift of the clock shall be less than 30 seconds per month. The battery shall operate maintenance-free for a period of at least 10 years. 9.6.2 The meter shall be equipped with a battery monitoring system that monitors the battery’s condition and sends a “Change Battery Alarm” message to the HES when the b attery approaches the end of its operating life 9.6.3 When first commissioned into the AMI System the meter shall synchronize its time & date with that of the HES. Meter time & date synchronization shall also be done on a periodic basis through the HES,

with a programmable periodicity, and done automatically during meter installation stage. 10. Power quality For low voltage meters (400V or less): 10.1. Meter shall record under voltage, overvoltage, and power cut. 10.2. Events related to under voltage and overvoltage shall be recorded in the meter. Thresh old of under voltage is

11 from 210 to 130 by 5V decrements and for overvoltage is from 230 to 285 by 5V increments 10.3. The v oltage e vents will n ot be rec orded unless they c ontinue f or a ti me equal to or great er than the programmable time set f or under voltage and overvoltage thresh old. This time shall be adjustable / programmable between 1 to 60 seconds, in 1-second increments. 10.4. For each under voltage, minimum voltage occurred during the adjustable / Programmable (1 to 60) Second period, the meter shall be detected and recorded as such; date and time of last occurrence and the cumulative number of the occurrences. 10.5. For each overvoltage, max. Voltage occurred and during the adjustable / Programmable (1 to

60) Second period the meter shall be detected and recorded as such; date and time of last occurrence and the cumulative number of the occurrences. 10.6. Parameters related to threshold and duration of under voltage and overvoltage shall be adjustable locally and remotely. 10.7. Events related to power cut shall be recorded in the meter as follows: a. For long duration power cuts (more than 3 min.), duration time and date shall be recorded. b. For short duration power cuts, number of power cuts shall be recorded. 11. Meter Display 11.1. Meter display shall display all measured quantities or desired quantity automatically or scrolling via button on the meter as follows: a. Active energy in each tariff

b. Reactive energy in each tariff c. Current tariff d. Max. Demand in each tariff, and the highest Max. Demand with exact time & date e. Time and date f. Serial number g. Battery failure h. The number of voltage cut offs and number of reverse energy events i. Contactor’s status (this feature will not be implemented in Transformer meters) j. All parts of item 3 (including instantaneous quantities, cumulative energy values, and maximum demand in all tariffs with time & date indication). 11.2. . Meter shall have capability of d isplay and record of current & pervious Max. Demand with ab ility of configuration of Max. Demand time interval calculation. Max. Demand shall be measured at least in three integer
and two decimal digits. 11.3. LCD shall display measured items in OBIS codes. 11.4. Meter Display shall support Arabic language Interface). 12. Fault & Status Display

12 The meter shall provide a visible indication of the status of the incoming supply. 12.1. Status/Alarm/Event Displays In addition to the displays required above, the meter must display the following minimum information in an intuitive way such that an inexperienced user can understand and interpret the information intelligibly (laminated instruction cards are to be provided for each unit): 1. Meter ID 2. Out of Credit message (for prepayment meters only) 3. Meter tamper state 4. Meter failure and/or fault code The bidder must submit a detailed specification of the way in which the displays are structured and laid out 13. Tamper detection The meter shall classify and record the following commonly-occurring tamper events with a date and time stamp. The tampering event log shall record any combination of the following possible tamper conditions: Table (8) Tamper shall be detected with and without mains power connections. When the meter detects a tamper condition, it shall be capable of configuring the latching relay/contactor to perform an action or not on the connected loads with an appropriate message. 16.1. There shall be a mechanism by which the consumer can be disconnected immediately when a v erifiable

tamper event occurs. 16.2. The tamper status shall be reset by using the meter optical port. 16.3. The meter shall send a push message to the Data Concentrator or even to the central system with every Tamper Event, stamped with date and time. 16.4. The meter shall provide tamper log data with the last occurred tamper date and time stamp and it can be easily readable remotely or locally. 17. Meter Metrology 1. The meter shall have the ability of measuring and recording import/export active energy (The values to be recorded for import and export are actual values). 2. The meter should have the ability of measuring and recording import/export reactive Energy (the values to be recorded for import and export are actual values).

16 3. The measuring quantity for energy shall have following resolutions: a. for Active Energy (Min 0.01 kWh resolution). b. for Reactive Energy (Min 0.01 kVARh resolution) 4. It shall be possible to locally and remotely select or configure exported energy whether to be registered or not.(according to standard, when the energy flows from network to the consumer it is registered as IMPORT and when the energy flows from consumer to the network it is registered as EXPORT.) 5. The meter is configured in such way not to register the consumed energy as Export (except Distributed Generations) and whenever it is occurred, the system shall record this event. 6. The meter shall measure import/export energy as well as Absolute value of active energy and it shall

be possible to activate this capability. 7. The meter shall measure phase current (True RMS) 8. The meter shall measure phase voltage (True RMS) 9. The meter shall measure Power Factor. 10. The measuring and recording maximum demand of active power shall be based on configurable time intervals and subintervals (sliding mode). a. It shall be possible to reset maximum demand remotely b. It shall be possible to reset maximum demand locally vi a sealed push button on front of the meter. c. It shall be possible automatically Maximum demand to be reset at specific billing Period. Above capability shall be activated (or not) and all parameter shall be adjustable remotely and locally. 11. The meter shall measure the energy from both phase and neutral (which one is larger) by same

accuracy. 12. Voltage sampling shall be done before input of the contactor/ latching relay. 13. Detailed OBIS code for the supplied meter should be offered . 18. Payment Mode 18.1. Most benefits of prepayment mode is that credit is transferred to the meter from online vending method, and the amount credit in local currency provided/ stored inside the meter, the meter will provide supply energy to the consumer when he have a enough credit. The credit register is decremented against energy consumption with the countdown total being displayed on the meter display. 18.2. When the consumer has consumed their credit amount, the meter shall shut off the supplied energy to the consumer until the meter has been recharged with more credit. This shutting off process will not be activated

during holidays, weekends, or defined “Friendly Hours” (utility non-working hours). 18.3. There shall be a mechanism to trigger Friendly Hours to block customer disconnection when AMI communication is lost for a definable period (in hours) but electric service is maintained so that the customer

17 will not lose service due to lack of credit. 18.4. For post-payment mode, the meter will calculate the consumed energy according to the tariff management system, and transfer into money payable value, which shall then be displayed on the meter display in a local currency value. 18.5. Under normal circumstances, the consumer will pay their bill within the defined grace period as indicated on the bill (or by some other mechanism). Notifications about payment due shall be via SMS message, paper bills, or Utility personnel, among others. In the case of non-payment over the period as determined by the Utility for their collections process and programmed into the meter (number of day of grace period), the

meter will shut off the supplied energy to the consumer. Optionally, there shall be a two-stage demand limiting function whereby the Utility can configure two lower demand limits to be managed by the meter and switch to encourage the customer to manage their consumption and ultimately pay for it. 18.6. Mandatory - The meter shall be capable to be switched from prepayment to post-payment mode remotely from the Master Information Center / Central System and locally via the optical port 19. Credit register (prepayment mode only) The credit register is the “operating account” of the meter being decremented by the metering process. The specific requirements of this register are: 19.1. This register must be decremented for the equivalent of every credit unit consumed.

19.2. When the credit reaches a pre-determined point, the customer shall be informed about the re maining balance and advised to purchase more credit. Optionally, a soft demand limit is enacted, permitting them to maintain service at a lower level to stretch the remaining credit until more is purchased. There shall be two programmable points to enable this process at two different load levels. 19.3. When the available credit has been consumed, the meter must open the contactor and isolate su pply from the customer (unless the Friendly hours are invoked). 19.4. The balance of this register shall be collected by the HES 20. Friendly Hours, Weekend & Holiday Features

18 Meter shall accommodate the “Friendly hours”, “Weekend”, and “Holidays” features. These are periods during which the meter shall not cut-off power to consumer if the credit becomes negative. The Friendly Hour mechanism shall also be configurable to be triggered due to loss of AMI communications for a defined length of time. Details of the features are described in the following table: Table (9) No. Requirement Description 1 Friendly Hours There shall be provision to define ‘Friendly hours’ in the meter. The utility shall be able to define the ‘Friendly hours’ and shall be communicated to the meter through token. 2 Weekend There shall be provision to define ‘Weekend’ in the meter. The utility shall be able to define the

weekend and shall be communicated to the meter through token. 3 Holidays There shall be provision to define ‘Holidays’ in the meter. At the end of a year, the utility shall be able to define new holiday dates and these shall be communicated to meter through 21. Tariff 21.1. All tariff calculation and credit calculation shall be processed inside the meter. 21.2. The meter shall support stepped tariffs structures as well as time-of-use and maximum demand. 21.3. The tariff structure consists of the following records: a) Each tariff must be uniquely identified using the tariff code b) Each tariff shall have an activation date, being the date on which the tariff becomes effective. c) Each tariff has up to ten steps / time slot (in kWh) for different levels of energy pricing.

d) Each tariff has up to ten steps / time slot (in kVARh) for different levels of energy pricing e) The rate describes the cost per kWh for energy consumption in that step. f) The rate describes the cost per kVARh for energy consumption in that step. 22. Tariff switchover When the meter detects that a new tariff is applicable (using the tariff activation date), the meter shall execute the following steps: a) The meter shall begin the billing against the new tariff. b) The current tariff code shall be updated to reflect the new tariff code. c) The old (expired) tariff should be deleted. d) New tariffs will only be activated at 00:00 on the first day of a month.

19 23. Billing Billing operates on a “monthly” basis (a period of days definable by the Utility). The energy consumption charge is deducted within the meter for at least every 1 kWh. All other charges including service charge, minimum charge, VAT, and other taxes are deducted at the POS. 24. Load Disconnection 24.1. The load shall be disconnected by a latching relay/contactor under the following conditions: a) The credit in the meter has expired and grace facilities have also expired b) The load power threshold has been exceeded c) The meter is in a tampered state 24.2. The meter shall indicate whether the load is connected or not and shall display the reason for disconnection. 24.3. Shall have the option to switch the contactor manually. Under over – current (reaching the load limit)

conditions by pushing a button in front of the meter, and also the mechanism shall permit automatic load reconnection and shall ensure that the number of switching cycles is limited. The meter shall attempt to reconnect the load up to programmable trail times with programmable time period intervals. If the over -current condition still exits the meter shall wait a programmable period of time before attempting to reconnect the load , and after the limited times of switching cycles Expiration the meter will not restore back the energy unless it done remotely from the HES or even locally by the Optical port. 25. Load Control Meters shall have the options to control loads that shall be remotely activated or deactivated with the

following features: Table (10) No. Requirement Description 1 Allowed loads for given time periods There should be provision in the meter to define the allowable loads f or given time periods within a day 2 Demand Limit A levels of load current amount in order to disconnect the latching relay/contactor for warning the consumer to recharge his meter ( prepaid mode) or even to pay the electricity bill (postpaid mode), which will be applied according to the remaining credit value 2 Load Limit step Load limit value shall be defined in step of 500 W.

Smart Meters - General Requirements Meter that connected through current and voltage transformer at medium voltage in aim to measure the total energy consumed from utility Distribution Company at certain point 1.1. Service Conditions The meters to be supplied according to this specification shall be suitable for satisfactory continuous operation under the following environmental conditions: Table (1) 1.2. Environmental Requirements The meters shall conform to the environmental requirements as defined in IEC 62052-11, 62053-21 and 62053-23. In addition to those requirements, the following minimum requirements shall be met: 1. The meter shall be protected against malfunction due to the ingress of vermin, dust, or excess

humidity by conformal coating of the printed circuit boards in the meter. 2. Any openings shall be as small as practically possible to prevent the ingress of dirt and vermin . and to limit the potential for vandalism or tamper following Meter case. 1.3. Standards and codes of practice All material and equipment supplied and all works carried out shall comply in every respect with the technical codes of the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and with the recommendations of the International Electro technical Commission (IEC) and the European Standards (EN), which apply to the electrical equipment. 7 1.5. General Functional Requirements 1.5.1. Meter Sealing 1. Provision shall be for sealing the meter with at least two Ferrule stainless steel seals 2. The stainless steel seals shall be applied in such a way that it will not be possible to undo/loosen the mounting screws used to secure the meter without breaking these seals. 3. The stainless steel seals shall be applied in such a way that they will be easily visible when viewing an installed meter from the front. 4. The terminals shall also be having Ferrule/TT sealing arrangement. 1.5.2. Smart Meter RS-485 Serial Communication Interface In this specification, RS-485 serial communication interface is used for physical layer Communication interface standard.

1.5.3. Meter Local Communication Interface 1. The meter shall have an IEC 62056 -21 compliant optical communication port. This shall be place to allow the Utility to access via the front of the meter for a variety of interactions via a hand held unit (HHU). 2. The meter shall have a 2-wire (+Ground Reference) serial communication port 3. The meter shall have an RS485 compatible with IEC 62056 communication protocol for a local connected network of 30 meters (without a repeat er) or Local connection with nearest data concentrator (RS gateway) in the field of installation. With one wide area network (WAN) Gateway. 1.5.4. Meter Remote Communication Interface - The meter shall have remote communication option which will be able to interface with RS -485, RJ-45

port for data communication with the central server from meters via one of the different communication media (RS-485 Serial Communication as the primary communication type). - Communication device shall be a modem usi ng GSM and 4G mobile network communication. In addition, or in replacement, it should be possible to connect an external modem (PSTN, GSM, 4G) - The communication modules works as a plug -and-play model, without the need to modify/change the meter firmware. - The communication protocol shall be compatible with the IEC 62056 Dynamic DLMS/COSEM Application Layer Protocol. DLMS Compliance Certificate (mandatory) ,The meter shall have a certificate for IEC62056 DLMS/COSEM, for the following:
- Optical port Communication - 2-wire Serial RS485 Communication port - RS-485 COMMUNICATION SPECIFICATION

8 - RS-485, also known as TIA-485(-A), EIA-485, is a standard defining the electrical characteristics of drivers and receivers for use in serial communications systems. Electrical signaling is balanced, and multipoint systems are supported. - The standard is jo intly published by the Telecommunications Industry Association and Electronic Industries Alliance (TIA/EIA). Digital communications networks implementing the standard can be used effectively over long distances and in electrically noisy environments. Firmware upgrade Mandatory - The Utility shall be able to update / change the meter firmware locally via the optical port or other ports installed on the meter. The firmware upgrade mechanism shall be designed so as not to alter in a ny way the metering

characteristics (metrology) or the meter data stored in the meter: 1. Meter data 2. Meter status 3. Configuration parameters or operational parameters All of these data must remain unchanged even after firmware upgrades. New firmware will be submitted to the meter with date and time parameter of e xecuting new firmware operation (the meter will upload the new updated firmware but will only execute it when the defined date and time parameter achieved). The meter will record the date and time of receipt of new firmware in the event log, as well as the date and

10 time of the firmware upgrade process execution. The meter will perform self-check process after the execution of the new firmware update, and the result of sel f-check process will be stored on the meter event log, and sh all be retrievable locally or remotely. The meter shall have non-volatile memory equal to or greater than ten ti mes the size of the operational/running firmware image. The meter shall support two concurrent firmware images: current operational/running and pending upgrade 1.5.6. Security The meters shall use a method for encryption/decryption of data exchanged in all meter interfaces. According to cyber security department penetration test. Mandatory - A method employing minimally AES128 shall be used in all information exchanges in private

network, between meter and RS gateway (Field Area Network, or FAN interface), and between the meter and hand held unit (HHU) via the optical interface. Enough re s e rve capacity in the components of the system (e.g., main processor, memory) shall exist to support AES256 during the service life of the equipment. The meter shall support encryption and decryption of two meter firmware images: current operational/running, pending upgrade. The meter should be capable of supporting AES256 during its operational lifetime without a hardware change. A method employing at least ECC192 shall be used in all information exchanges on public network, as well as between meter and central system directly. The security methods and hardware shall be described in details by bidder.

The bidder shall prove following items: 1. Security of method 2. Client ability to change security key remotely 3. Confidentiality of remote key changing 4. If client changes the security key there is no way for meter manufacturers to retrieve new key (new key is not accessible for meter hardware, firmware, and external software) 5. All input and output information decrypted and encrypted 1.5.7. Meter Operational Modes The meter shall be required to support at least two modes of operation: Normal Mode and Test Mode. 1.5.8. Meter Test Mode The meter shall support a Test Mode that is activated by using the optical port. In Test Mode, there shall be an automated test sequence available that includes:

11 1. Full diagnostic test, that testing of the all the active and inactive functionality 2. Metering accuracy test (enabled to allow for accuracy testing to be performed without affecting the recorded customer registration for energy) 3. Connection validation tests (three-phase sequence connection). The Test Mode shall also have the following displayed information (scrolled sequence): 1. Meter firmware version 2. Meter firmware update date and time stamp 3. Meter tampering events 4. Current limit 5. Connect/ disconnect 1.5.9. Load Profile 1. At least four programmable channels for: a. Active interval energy (import and export) b. Reactive interval energy (import and export) * c. Active cumulative energy (import and export)

d. Reactive cumulative energy (import and export) * Adjustable time interval from 1 to 60 minutes. Meter shall store these parameters for at least 30 days. 2. At least four programmable channels for voltage and current as well as active and reactive power. Measurement type for each channel can be chosen as follows: a. average b. minimum c. maximum Adjustable time interval f rom 1 to 60 minutes, Meter shall store these parameters for at least 30 days. 3. Time & date shall be included in load profile. 4. Load profile shall be readable in complete form and in defined blocks (based on start and end time and channels) locally and remotely.

12 1.5.10. Historic Consumption For this option, there are twelve energy registers that st ore the total energy consumed for the previous twelve months. These registers are cycled at during the month-end process, with the oldest data being deleted. 1.5.11. Date and time management The meter shall be equipped with a real time clock supporting the date and time. Pseudo-clock using mains crossing detection is not acceptable. The maximum drift of the clock shall be less than 30 seconds per month. The battery shall operate maintenance-free for a period of at least 10 years. The meter shall be equipped with a battery monitoring system that monitors the battery’s condition and sends a “Change Battery Alarm” message to the HES when the battery approaches the end of its operating

life When first commissioned into the AMI System the meter shall synchronize its time & date with that of the HES. Meter time & date synchronization shall also be done on a periodic basis through the HES, with a programmable periodicity, and done automatically during meter installation stage. 1.5.12. Power quality 1. Meter shall record under voltage, overvoltage, and power cut. 2. Events related to under voltage and overvoltage shall be recorded in the meter. Threshold of under voltage is from 110 to 66 by 5V increments and for overvoltage is from 110 to 143 by 5V increments 3. The voltage events will not be recorded unless they continue for a time equal to or greater than the programmable time set for under voltage and overvoltage threshold. This time shall be

adjustable / programmable between 1 to 60 seconds, in 1-second increments. 4. For each under voltage, minimum voltage occurred during the adjustable / Programmable (1 to 60) Second period, the meter shall be detected and recorded as such; date and time of last occurrence and the cumulative number of the occurrences. 5. For each overvoltage, max. Voltage occurred and during the adjustable / Programmable (1 to 60) Second period the meter shall be detected and recorded as such; date and time of last occurrence and the cumulative number of the occurrences. 6. Parameters related to threshold and duration of under voltage and overvoltage shall be adjustable locally and remotely. 7. Events related to power cut shall be recorded in the meter as follows:

a. For long duration power cuts (more than 3 min.), duration time and date shall be recorded. b. For short duration power cuts, number of power cuts shall be recorded. 1.5.13. Meter Display 1. Meter display shall display all measured quantities or desired quantity automatically or scrolling via button on the meter as follows:

13 a. Active energy in each quadrant b. Reactive energy in each quadrant c. Active energy in first& fourth quadrant d. Reactive energy in first & second quadrant e. Current Max. Demand and the Max. Demand with exact time & date monthly & quarterly f. Time and date g. Serial number h. Battery failure i. The number of voltage cut offs and number of reverse energy events j. Cell code no k. CT. & VT ratio l. average power factor (monthly) 2. Meter shall have capability of display and record of Max. Demand with ability of configuration of Max. Demand time interval calculation. Max. The meter shall measure phase current (True RMS) in A 4. The meter shall measure phase voltage (True RMS) in V 5. The meter shall measure Power Factor 6. The meter shall measure Load profile 7. 8. It shall be possible to locally and remotely select or configure exported energy whether to be registered or not.(according to standard, when the energy flows from network to the consumer it is registered as IMPORT and when the energy flows from consumer to the network it is registered as EXPORT.) 9. The meter is configured in such way not to register the consumed energy as Export (except Distributed Generations) and whenever it is occurred, the system shall record this event. 10. The meter shall measure import/export energy as well as Absolute value of active energy and it shall be possible to activate this capability. 11. The measuring and recording maximum demand of active power shall be based on

configurable time intervals and subintervals (sliding mode). a. It shall be possible to reset maximum demand remotely b. The meter shall have the ability to measure Maximum Demand 14. 15. Note: units on active energy (Wh), reactive energy (VARh), and demand (W) shall be configurable as kilo or Mega 6. Tariff (For Private Feeders /transformer) 6.1.1. All tariff calculation and credit calculation shall be processed inside the meter. 6.1.2. The meter shall support stepped tariffs structures as well as time-of-use and maximum demand. 6.1.3. The tariff structure consists of the following records: 1. Each tariff must be uniquely identified using the tariff code 2. Each tariff shall have an activation date, being the date on which the tariff becomes

17 effective. 3. Each tariff has up to ten steps / time slot (in kWh) for different levels of energy pricing. 4. Each tariff has up to ten steps / time slot (in kVARh) for different levels of energy pricing. 5. The rate describes the cost per kWh for energy consumption in that slot. 6. The rate describes the cost per kVARh for energy consumption in that slot. 6.1.4. Tariff switchover When the meter detects that a new tariff is applicable (using the tariff activation date), the meter shall execute the following steps: . 1. The meter shall begin the billing against the new tariff. 2. The current tariff code shall be updated to reflect the new tariff code. 3. The old (expired) tariff should be deleted.

4. New tariffs will only be activated at 00:00 on the first day of a month. 7. Billing Billing operates on a “monthly” basis (a period of days definable by the Utility). The energy consumption charge is deducted within the meter for at least every 1 kWh. All other charges including service charge, minimum charge, VAT, and other taxes are deducted at the POS. 8. Page 3 of 26 1. SCOPE Many important business processes conducted by the electricity distribution companies (EDCs) require re-engineering to increase effectiveness and efficiency. An important enabler for this re -engineering is an advanced metering i nfrastructure (AMI). The functionality of the AMI can contribute to the business needs identified by EEHC. Smart meters is the key role in AMI. Smart meters shall communicate using field area network (FAN) telecommunications technologies such as PLC, PLC/RF, mobile wireless service 2G/3G / 4G technology, and RS 485. The choice of FAN telecommunication technology shall optimize system design, architecture, interoperability and total cost of ownership

while meeting this specification. This specification describes the minimum technical requirements for design ing, manufacturing, testing, inspection, supply and delivery of three -phase indirect smart meters. The required meters shall be suitable for outdoor installations of the power networks of _ _EDC. 2.
